evidence_quotes.quote: especially when it comes to video games and other digital products.
when we buy a game online, were not actually buying the product itselfwere buying a license to use it.
Once someone buys a digital product, they should have the right to keep using itespecially the version they originally paid for.
It shouldnt be possible for a company to take it away or change it significantly after the fact, unless the user agrees.
No silent removal or remote disabling
Were seeing more and more cases where companies remove games from stores or even deactivate them remotely, making them unplayableeven for people who already bought them.
3. Offline access matters If a digital product depends on servers or online features, there should always be a way to keep using it when those services end.
This could mean offering an offline mode, releasing a patch, or providing DRM-free versions.
4. Be clear about time limits If a product is only going to work for a limited time, that needs to be clearly communicated before someone buys it.
Hidden time limits or surprise shutdowns damage trust and should not be allowed.
5. Give consumers real protection The Digital Fairness Act should make sure companies cant just remove access to paid content without consequences.
There should be refunds, legal options, or alternative ways to keep using what was purchased.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, Im writing to share some concerns about digital ownership and consumer rights, especially when it comes to video games and other digital products. These days, when we buy a game online, were not actually buying the product itselfwere buying a license to use it. That might seem like a technical detail, but it has serious consequences. As someone whos built up a large digital collection of games over the years, I worry that I could lose access to everything Ive paid for at any momentjust because a company decides to shut something down or remove access. Here are a few points I hope the Digital Fairness Act will take into account: 1. People should be able to keep what they paid for Once someone buys a digital product, they should have the right to keep using itespecially the version they originally paid for. It shouldnt be possible for a company to take it away or change it significantly after the fact, unless the user agrees. 2. No silent removal or remote disabling Were seeing more and more cases where companies remove games from stores or even deactivate them remotely, making them unplayableeven for people who already bought them. That kind of action feels unfair and should be clearly regulated. 3. Offline access matters If a digital product depends on servers or online features, there should always be a way to keep using it when those services end. This could mean offering an offline mode, releasing a patch, or providing DRM-free versions. People shouldnt lose access just because a company decides to move on. 4. Be clear about time limits If a product is only going to work for a limited time, that needs to be clearly communicated before someone buys it. Hidden time limits or surprise shutdowns damage trust and should not be allowed. 5. Give consumers real protection The Digital Fairness Act should make sure companies cant just remove access to paid content without consequences. There should be refunds, legal options, or alternative ways to keep using what was purchased. Digital products are a big part of everyday life now, and its important that consumer rights keep up. People deserve to feel secure in their purchaseswhether theyre buying a physical item or a digital one. Thank you for taking the time to read this and for working on a fairer digital future. Sincerely, Rens Elands
